identity.title: Other Aircraft Parts and Auxiliary Equipment Manufacturing

lens.included_activities: Independent LMM manufacturers and approved suppliers of aircraft parts, subassemblies, auxiliary equipment, modification and replacement articles, and prototypes sold repeatedly to external OEMs, airlines, maintenance organizations, defense customers, or distributors.
lens.excluded_activities: Aircraft engines and fluid-power subassemblies classified elsewhere, captive plants, pure distributors, maintenance-only firms, shells, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat qualified B2B program, spares, replacement, and prototype-to-production revenue supported by engineering, configuration control, inspection, traceability, certification, and customer quality systems.

a.bridge: BLS production profiles and adjacent fabricated-metal data describe machining and assembly work rather than six-digit 336413 wage-weighted tasks, while FAA sources document approval and traceability workflows. I map requirements review, quoting, engineering search, configuration and quality documentation, inspection-data analysis, scheduling, procurement, and customer support as exposed; precision machining, forming, assembly, nondestructive testing, inspection execution, maintenance, and material handling are less substitutable. The proxy may overstate exposure because certification requires accountable review.
a.rationale: Aerospace suppliers have unusually document- and engineering-intensive workflows, but precision physical production and inspection remain a large share of labor.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted task inventories from 10-15 approved LMM suppliers, including current ERP, PLM, QMS, CAD/CAM, and inspection automation, would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: Defense and commercial documentation burdens differ.

rho.rationale: Document search, quote preparation, planning, quality trending, nonconformance support, and engineering assistance can be implemented within five years. FAA approvals, configuration control, validation, cybersecurity, export controls, customer qualification, and catastrophic error costs constrain deployment.
rho.change_evidence: Audited pilots measuring hours saved, escapes, rework, approval cycle time, and human review across quoting, engineering change, quality, and traceability workflows would move the range.
caveats: Bounded judgment rather than observed adoption.
AI outputs cannot replace formal engineering and airworthiness accountability.

e.rationale: Independent qualified parts manufacturers generally have repeat external program or spares revenue, but captive suppliers, project-only prototype shops, defense restrictions, weak approvals, single-OEM concentration, poor quality history, and non-transferable customer certifications reduce eligibility.
e.change_evidence: Screen the injected 76 candidates using FAA production approvals, IAQG OASIS, SAM.gov, business databases, ownership, customer concentration, and program-level revenue.
caveats: Injected firm count uses an auto-parts margin bridge and is estimated.
Classification does not prove FAA approval, independence, or LMM economics.

s5.bridge: Federal Reserve owner-age chartbooks cover small businesses broadly rather than aerospace parts transfers. I use owner age as succession pressure and discount for internal succession, closures, minority deals, approvals and customer requalification, export controls, environmental diligence, and strategic subsidiaries. Supplier consolidation may raise strategic acquisitions, while quality or concentration risk may prevent transfers.
s5.rationale: Succession and OEM supply-chain consolidation create deals, but buyer qualification, customer consent, program concentration, and compliance slow or derail qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook/CapIQ transactions, FAA approval-holder ownership changes, OASIS supplier histories, and aerospace M&A adviser interviews since 2018 would provide a denominator-based estimate.

q.rationale: Qualification, traceability, switching and revalidation costs, low-volume complexity, and spares urgency protect savings. Large OEM cost-down programs, open-book contracts, competitive sourcing, and long-term agreement repricing pass some gains through.
q.change_evidence: Long-term agreements, OEM cost-down clauses, bid histories, spares pricing, margin by program, qualification costs, and customer interviews would directly measure retention.
caveats: No public contract sample quantifies pass-through.
PMA spares and proprietary equipment likely retain more than build-to-print OEM parts.

d5.bridge: FAA forecasts U.S. commercial aircraft fleet growth from 6,949 in 2025 to 10,677 in 2046, a broader long-horizon stock measure rather than five-year 336413 demand. I translate the fleet and activity expansion into moderate five-year parts, production, and spares growth, with supply constraints and defense or general-aviation mix widening the range. A growing fleet may overstate new-part demand if utilization or retirements differ.
d5.rationale: Commercial fleet expansion, aging-aircraft maintenance, defense and modification needs, and production backlogs support real growth, while OEM disruptions, program cancellations, and airline cycles create downside.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year OEM build schedules, airline fleet and maintenance plans, defense procurement, PMA approvals, and target-specific backlog by program would materially revise the estimate.
caveats: FAA fleet forecast is not a direct industry shipment forecast.
Target program concentration dominates aggregate demand.

o.rationale: Retained demand requires an approved accountable manufacturer to produce, inspect, trace, and certify physical aircraft parts. Software and additive manufacturing change methods but do not eliminate the responsible production organization; some airlines may fabricate limited articles internally.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on airline internal fabrication, OEM insourcing, certified additive production, and shifts in FAA approval-holder structure would move the operator-required share.
caveats: Operator share can migrate to OEMs or other approved suppliers.
This is distinct from internal labor automation.

narrative.executive_view: Aircraft parts combine high labor intensity, dense engineering and quality documentation, strong qualification barriers, and favorable fleet demand. AI can improve knowledge workflows, but certification and traceability constrain autonomous implementation.
narrative.ai_impact: Likely uses include requirements and drawing review, quote preparation, engineering search, configuration documentation, inspection-data analysis, nonconformance support, scheduling, procurement, and customer quality responses. Machining, forming, assembly, NDT, inspection execution, and release remain physical and accountable.
narrative.value_capture: Qualification, traceability, revalidation expense, program knowledge, and spares urgency support retention. OEM cost-downs, long-term agreements, open-book economics, and competitive sourcing still share benefits.
narrative.firm_availability: Most independent qualified manufacturers fit repeat external supply, but approvals, program concentration, customer consent, export controls, quality history, and strategic ownership materially shrink transferable supply.
narrative.demand_durability: Fleet expansion, aging-aircraft maintenance, defense programs, modifications, and OEM backlogs support parts demand. Program cancellations, OEM production interruptions, airline cycles, and customer concentration remain material.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Certification, product liability, quality escapes, counterfeit or untraceable material, export controls, cybersecurity, customer concentration, long qualification cycles, and margin-bridge uncertainty dominate.
narrative.principal_driver: Qualification and traceability create sticky repeat revenue around growing fleet and spares demand.
narrative.principal_weakness: Certification accountability and concentrated OEM programs constrain implementation and transferability.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: Other Aircraft Parts and Auxiliary Equipment
sources.supports: Census defines 336413 as manufacturing or prototyping aircraft parts and auxiliary equipment other than engines and aircraft fluid-power subassemblies.

sources.title: FAA Production Approvals
sources.supports: FAA states that production certificates authorize duplicate products under approved type designs and that PMA combines design and production approval for modification and replacement articles.

sources.title: Assemblers and Fabricators
sources.supports: BLS describes physical alignment and assembly tasks and specifically notes physical demands for aerospace assemblers.

sources.title: FAA Aerospace Forecast FY 2026-2046
sources.supports: FAA forecasts commercial aircraft increasing from 6,949 in 2025 to 10,677 in 2046, used as a fleet-demand proxy.

sources.title: Acquisition of FAA-Certified Consumable Parts
sources.supports: Federal acquisition rules require approval checks and an unbroken lot, batch, or serial-number traceability chain for relevant FAA-certified parts.

sources.title: 2024 Chartbook on Firms by Age of Primary Owner
sources.supports: Federal Reserve owner-age evidence used only as a broad succession proxy.
